items.rationale: No agent registry or named owner is visible in the code — this lives outside the repo.
A credential read from the environment flows straight into an outbound HTTP request at src/legacy/utils.py:328 — a possible data-egress path worth verifying.
The pinned dependencies include known-vulnerable versions (for example langchain and pygments) with open advisories.
No human-approval step is visible around the agent's high-consequence actions.
Whether a tested kill-switch exists can't be seen from the code.

items.plain_explanation: No list of your production agents, or a named owner for each, shows up in the code — that lives in a doc or a spreadsheet.
items.fix: Keep one table of every agent you run, each with a single named owner, and add a row before any new agent ships.

items.plain_explanation: At src/legacy/utils.py:328 a value read from the environment flows straight into an outbound web request. If that value is a credential, this is a path for it to leave your systems.
items.fix: Confirm what is being sent, and make sure an agent's privileges come from your configuration, not from data it reads.

items.plain_explanation: Several pinned dependencies have known security advisories open against them — for example langchain and pygments. Your agent inherits every one of those.
items.fix: Run a dependency scan on a schedule and upgrade the flagged packages; treat critical advisories as work to close, not noise.

items.plain_explanation: Nothing in the code shows a person approving high-consequence actions before they run. For an agent that can reach the network and the shell, that is the highest-leverage control to add.
items.fix: Route high-consequence actions to a human to approve before they fire — a Slack message or a review queue is enough to start.

items.plain_explanation: Whether you can stop this agent on demand isn't something the code can show.
items.fix: Make sure one command or button disables the agent's credentials, and test it on a schedule so it works when you need it.
